refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing 'how' (e.g., redundancy, error correction, protocol selection) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'communication integrity' is more precise than 'communication quality' and encompasses both packet loss and related reliability concerns without naming specific mechanisms.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profiles, interference scenarios) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate. Confirm that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Log packet statistics at the application layer to distinguish network-induced loss from application-layer filtering or retransmission.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of heavy equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data transmission with packet loss not exceeding the customer-defined limit under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and customer-configurable thresholds
DP2: Employ forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tied to measured network conditions
DP3: Design a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover and load balancing across independent network routes
DP4: Implement application-level acknowledgment and timeout-driven resend logic with exponential backoff and customer-tunable parameters
DP5: Combine network-layer QoS prioritization with application-layer packet prioritization to protect critical teleoperation commands
DP6: Use a hybrid approach: FEC for continuous telemetry streams + selective retransmission for critical control commands, with customer-defined packet classification
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Implement a hybrid integrity strategy combining forward error correction (FEC) for continuous telemetry streams and selective retransmission for critical control commands, with customer-configurable packet classification and loss thresholds. The strategy shall include real-time loss monitoring, adaptive redundancy adjustment, and fallback to safe operational state if integrity targets cannot be maintained.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between packet classification logic and command semantics—mitigated by defining classification as a customer-configurable policy external to core codec
Dependency on accurate real-time loss measurement—mitigated by isolating loss detection as a separate monitoring module with well-defined interfaces
FEC and retransmission logic may compete for bandwidth—mitigated by explicit trade-off parameters exposed to customer configuration
Safe state fallback requires coordination with operational control layer—mitigated by defining fallback as a discrete, testable state transition independent of recovery mechanism
coupling_check.notes: DP6 avoids technology lock-in (no specific codec, protocol, or vendor named) and maintains logical separation between detection, classification, correction, and fallback. Coupling is manageable through clear module boundaries and customer-configurable policies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer can define packet loss target (≤1% or alternative) and network conditions under which it applies; RISK: Vague or changing acceptance criteria will invalidate design validation.
ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands can be classified into critical vs. non-critical; RISK: Misclassification or inability to classify in real-time may compromise safety or efficiency.
ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are measurable and relatively stable during agreed operational windows; RISK: Sudden, unpredictable network degradation may exceed adaptive capacity.

ASSUMPTION: FEC overhead and retransmission latency are acceptable within teleoperation control loop timing; RISK: Excessive latency or bandwidth consumption may degrade operator experience or violate safety timing constraints.
ASSUMPTION: Safe operational state (fallback) is well-defined and achievable without external intervention; RISK: Ambiguous or unreachable safe state may leave system in unsafe condition.
ASSUMPTION: Customer network infrastructure supports QoS tagging or dual-path routing if needed; RISK: Legacy or constrained networks may not support required features.
ASSUMPTION: Loss detection mechanism is reliable and does not introduce false positives; RISK: Incorrect loss detection triggers unnecessary retransmission or FEC, wasting bandwidth.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'agreed network conditions' (latency range, jitter, baseline loss rate, bandwidth); (2) Packet classification criteria (which commands/telemetry are critical); (3) Acceptable latency budget for retransmission and FEC overhead; (4) Definition and testability of 'safe operational state' fallback; (5) Measurement and reporting mechanism for actual packet loss during operation. This will enable DP6 to be decomposed into independent, testable sub-DPs (loss detection, FEC codec, retransmission scheduler, packet classifier, fallback controller) with clear ownership boundaries (IMs) and acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for integrity assurance, loss monitoring, adaptive redundancy, and safe-state fallback mechanisms across all teleoperation and vehicle communication pathways.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous telemetry streams from Vehicle; Safety Services applies FEC, monitors packet loss, and classifies packets per customer configuration.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives critical control commands from Teleoperation; Safety Services applies selective retransmission logic, validates integrity targets, and returns acknowledgment or safe-state directives.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits alerts when integrity targets cannot be maintained; triggers fallback to safe operational state across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interacts with network layer to apply redundancy adjustments, monitor real-time loss metrics, and configure packet classification thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of integrity assurance, loss monitoring, adaptive redundancy, and safe-state fallback. FEC and retransmission logic are core safety concerns. Customer-configurable thresholds align with safety policy management. Minor diffusion: Network Link interface required for real-time metrics and redundancy adjustment; Telemetry and Tasking interfaces are well-defined boundaries.

Weak fit. Teleoperation is a consumer of integrity assurance, not its owner. FEC and retransmission logic would diffuse across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Network Link. Safe-state fallback requires coordination outside Teleoperation scope. Loss monitoring and adaptive redundancy are cross-system concerns, not Teleoperation-specific.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a data source and actuator, not an integrity arbiter. FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission logic would diffuse across Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Network Link. Safe-state fallback requires Vehicle to execute commands, but integrity strategy ownership belongs upstream. Loss monitoring and adaptive redundancy are system-level concerns.

Moderate fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level container that could house Safety Services as a subsystem. However, assigning DP6 directly to NGHE Solution is overly broad and obscures ownership. Better to assign to Safety Services (a child of NGHE Solution) for clarity and cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the most specific and cohesive boundary for DP6. It owns the integrity assurance strategy, real-time loss monitoring, adaptive redundancy logic, and safe-state fallback mechanisms. The module interfaces cleanly with Telemetry (input), Tasking (input/output), SafetyAlert (output), and Network Link (bidirectional for metrics and configuration). Customer-configurable packet classification and loss thresholds are safety policy parameters, appropriately managed by Safety Services. Diffusion is minimal because FEC, retransmission, and fallback logic are safety-critical concerns that should not be scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Network Link.
containment_risks: Real-time loss monitoring and adaptive redundancy adjustment require tight coupling to Network Link; latency in metric propagation could delay redundancy adaptation.
Customer-configurable thresholds must be validated and persisted; unclear if Safety Services owns configuration storage or delegates to a separate configuration management module.
Safe-state fallback requires coordinated action across Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services must emit clear SafetyAlert signals and ensure both subsystems respond atomically.
FEC encoding/decoding is computationally intensive; if Safety Services is software-only, performance bottlenecks may emerge on resource-constrained platforms.
Selective retransmission logic depends on accurate loss classification; if packet classification is misconfigured by customer, integrity targets may be unachievable, creating false alarms.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP6 is well-formulated and maps cleanly to Safety Services. No revision needed. However, consider decomposing DP6 into sub-DPs if implementation reveals tight coupling between FEC (continuous streams) and retransmission (critical commands): DP6a (FEC for telemetry) and DP6b (selective retransmission for tasking) could be owned by Safety Services but implemented as separate sub-modules (e.g., TelemetryIntegrityHandler and TaskingIntegrityHandler) to improve modularity and testability. This would not change the IM boundary (still Safety Services) but would clarify internal architecture.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions) is measurable and testable. Safety Services module can emit real-time loss metrics and SafetyAlert signals that directly validate FR compliance. Test harnesses can inject controlled packet loss and verify FEC/retransmission behavior and safe-state fallback triggering. Worksite safety plan integration is addressable through SafetyAlert interface and safe-state fallback coordination.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to Safety Services (IM boundary) via DP6. The mapping is explicit: FR (maintain integrity within customer limits) → DP6 (hybrid FEC + selective retransmission + monitoring + adaptive redundancy + fallback) → Safety Services (centralized ownership). No ambiguity about which module owns the integrity strategy or how it interfaces with Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, and SafetyAlert.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP6 fully addresses FR. It specifies both the mechanism (FEC for continuous streams, selective retransmission for critical commands) and the control logic (real-time loss monitoring, adaptive redundancy adjustment, fallback to safe state). DP6 is customer-configurable (packet classification, loss thresholds) and operationally grounded (agreed network conditions, safe operational state). No logical gaps between FR and DP6.

diffusion_risks: Real-time loss monitoring and adaptive redundancy adjustment require tight coupling to Network Link for metric propagation. Latency in metric delivery could delay redundancy adaptation and violate packet loss targets. Recommend explicit SLA on Network Link metric interface (e.g., loss metrics delivered within 100 ms).

Customer-configurable packet classification and loss thresholds must be validated, persisted, and reloaded on system restart. Unclear if Safety Services owns configuration storage or delegates to a separate configuration management module. Recommend explicit ownership boundary: either Safety Services owns a local configuration cache, or a dedicated ConfigurationManager module provides thread-safe access with validation hooks.

Safe-state fallback requires atomic coordination across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. If SafetyAlert signal is lost or misinterpreted, Vehicle and Teleoperation may enter inconsistent states. Recommend explicit fallback protocol: SafetyAlert includes sequence number and checksum; Vehicle and Teleoperation acknowledge receipt and confirm safe-state entry before resuming normal operation.

FEC encoding/decoding and selective retransmission logic are computationally intensive. If Safety Services is implemented as a single software module on resource-constrained platforms, CPU/memory bottlenecks may emerge, delaying loss detection and redundancy adjustment. Recommend performance profiling and potential hardware acceleration or multi-threaded decomposition (DP6a/DP6b sub-modules).

Selective retransmission depends on accurate packet classification. If customer misconfigures classification rules (e.g., marking non-critical packets as critical), integrity targets may become unachievable, creating false SafetyAlert alarms and unnecessary safe-state fallbacks. Recommend validation logic in Safety Services: warn customer if configured thresholds are mathematically unachievable given observed loss rates and classification rules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural and cohesive owner of DP6. FEC, retransmission, loss monitoring, adaptive redundancy, and fallback logic are all safety-critical concerns that belong in a centralized integrity assurance module, not scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Network Link. Interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (in), Tasking (inout), SafetyAlert (out), Network Link (inout). Minor diffusion risk is Network Link coupling for real-time metrics, which is unavoidable and acceptable given the safety-critical nature of loss monitoring. No evidence of DP6 leaking into other modules. Recommended sub-decomposition (DP6a/DP6b) would improve internal modularity without changing IM boundary.

recommended_revisions: Add explicit SLA to Network Link interface: loss metrics must be delivered to Safety Services within 100 ms (or customer-defined latency budget) to ensure adaptive redundancy adjustment meets packet loss targets.

Clarify configuration ownership: either Safety Services owns a validated configuration cache (with reload/persistence logic), or delegate to a dedicated ConfigurationManager module with explicit validation hooks for customer-configurable thresholds. Document validation rules (e.g., warn if configured loss threshold is mathematically unachievable).

Define fallback protocol: SafetyAlert signal must include sequence number, checksum, and timestamp. Vehicle and Teleoperation must acknowledge receipt and confirm safe-state entry before resuming normal operation. Recommend timeout mechanism: if Vehicle or Teleoperation does not acknowledge within 500 ms, Safety Services escalates to emergency shutdown.

Decompose DP6 into sub-DPs for clarity and testability: DP6a (FEC for continuous telemetry streams) and DP6b (selective retransmission for critical control commands). Both remain owned by Safety Services but can be implemented as separate sub-modules (TelemetryIntegrityHandler and TaskingIntegrityHandler) to improve modularity, reduce coupling, and enable independent testing.

Add performance profiling requirement: measure CPU/memory overhead of FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission logic on target platforms. If overhead exceeds 10% of available resources, recommend hardware acceleration or multi-threaded decomposition.

Document customer configuration validation: Safety Services must validate packet classification rules and loss thresholds at configuration time. If rules are inconsistent or unachievable, emit warning and recommend corrected configuration to customer.

decision.justification: Gate results show all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence (containment score 0.92, verification feasibility high, allocation clarity clear). However, the diffusion_risks and recommended_revisions identify six concrete sub-decomposition opportunities that are safety-critical and architecturally significant. Given high safety criticality, the complexity of DP6 (hybrid FEC + selective retransmission + monitoring + adaptive redundancy + fallback), and the explicit recommendation to decompose into DP6a/DP6b sub-modules, proceeding to next-level sub-FRs is justified. This decomposition will improve modularity, reduce coupling, enable independent testing, and address performance/configuration/coordination risks before detailed design. Stopping here would leave safety-critical concerns (e.g., fallback protocol atomicity, configuration validation, real-time metric SLA) unresolved at the architectural level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR6.1: Maintain integrity of continuous telemetry streams (sensor data, vehicle state) through forward error correction and real-time loss monitoring, ensuring loss ≤ customer-defined target under agreed network conditions.

FR6.2: Maintain integrity of critical control commands (tasking directives, emergency stops) through selective retransmission with acknowledgment, ensuring loss ≤ customer-defined target and command delivery latency ≤ customer-defined budget.
FR6.3: Detect packet loss in real-time and adapt redundancy strategy (FEC strength, retransmission frequency) within 100 ms to maintain integrity targets without exceeding network bandwidth budget.

FR6.4: Validate and persist customer-configurable packet classification rules and loss thresholds, warn if configuration is mathematically unachievable, and reload on system restart without data loss.

FR6.5: Coordinate safe-state fallback atomically across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems via SafetyAlert signal with sequence number, checksum, and acknowledgment protocol, ensuring no inconsistent states if SafetyAlert is lost or delayed.

FR6.6: Execute FEC encoding/decoding and selective retransmission logic within CPU/memory budget (≤10% of available resources on target platforms) to avoid bottlenecks in loss detection and redundancy adjustment.

revise.why: DP6 is logically sound but requires explicit sub-DP articulation to address safety-critical concerns identified in diffusion_risks. Recommend revising DP6 to explicitly decompose into DP6a (FEC for continuous telemetry streams with real-time loss monitoring and adaptive redundancy) and DP6b (selective retransmission for critical control commands with acknowledgment and fallback coordination). Additionally, add explicit sub-DPs for configuration validation (DP6c) and performance budgeting (DP6d). This revision will clarify ownership boundaries, interface contracts (e.g., Network Link metric SLA, SafetyAlert protocol), and testability criteria before implementation.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss targets and network conditions are stable and measurable (not subject to frequent change).
Network Link module can reliably deliver loss metrics to Safety Services within 100 ms latency budget.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can reliably receive and acknowledge SafetyAlert signals within 500 ms timeout window.
Target platforms have sufficient CPU/memory headroom (≥10% available) for FEC and retransmission logic without hardware acceleration.
Customer configuration validation rules are deterministic and can be pre-computed at configuration time (not runtime-dependent).
Safe-state fallback is a well-defined operational state that Vehicle and Teleoperation can enter atomically without external coordination.
open_risks: Real-time metric propagation latency from Network Link to Safety Services could exceed 100 ms SLA under high network load, delaying adaptive redundancy adjustment and violating packet loss targets. Mitigation: establish explicit SLA contract with Network Link; implement metric buffering and priority queuing in Safety Services.

Configuration validation logic may not catch all edge cases (e.g., customer misconfigures packet classification rules that are logically inconsistent but individually valid). Mitigation: implement heuristic validation (e.g., warn if configured loss threshold is mathematically unachievable given observed loss rates) and provide customer feedback loop.

SafetyAlert fallback protocol may fail if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystem is unresponsive or in a degraded state. Mitigation: implement timeout mechanism and escalation to emergency shutdown; add watchdog timer to detect unresponsive subsystems.

FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission logic may consume excessive CPU/memory on resource-constrained platforms, creating bottlenecks in loss detection and redundancy adjustment. Mitigation: profile performance on target platforms; consider hardware acceleration or multi-threaded decomposition if overhead exceeds 10%.

Configuration persistence and reload logic may introduce data loss or corruption if system crashes during configuration update. Mitigation: implement atomic write-ahead logging and validation on reload.

Selective retransmission depends on accurate packet classification; misconfiguration could create false SafetyAlert alarms and unnecessary safe-state fallbacks, reducing system availability. Mitigation: validate classification rules at configuration time and provide customer guidance on rule design.
evidence_needed: Explicit SLA contract between Safety Services and Network Link specifying loss metric delivery latency (e.g., ≤100 ms) and metric accuracy (e.g., ±5% error tolerance).
Configuration validation algorithm and heuristic rules for detecting unachievable loss thresholds given observed loss rates and packet classification rules.
Fallback protocol specification including SafetyAlert signal format (sequence number, checksum, timestamp), acknowledgment mechanism, timeout window (e.g., 500 ms), and escalation logic.
Performance profiling results on target platforms showing CPU/memory overhead of FEC encoding/decoding and retransmission logic under various network loss conditions (0%, 5%, 10%, 20% loss).
Configuration persistence and reload logic with atomic write-ahead logging and validation on restart.
Test harness for injecting controlled packet loss and verifying FEC/retransmission behavior, adaptive redundancy adjustment, and safe-state fallback triggering.
Customer configuration guidance document with examples of valid/invalid packet classification rules and loss thresholds.
